revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Himegami]][[fr:Himégami]][[ja:比売神]][[zh:比賣神]]
{{Short description|Japanese goddess}}
{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | spouse = [[Ame-no-Koyane]] | children = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}} }} '''Himegami''' (比売神) is a Japanese deity worshipped at [[Kasuga-taisha]].<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a">{{Cite web |date=2022-08-17 |title=Himegami {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9952 |access-date=2023-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220817065706/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9952 |archive-date=2022-08-17 }}</ref> It may refer to a specific goddess or an unknown female goddess. The male equivalent is Hikogami (比古神).<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
She is the wife of [[Ame-no-Koyane]].<ref name="ten-grotenhuis-1999a">{{Cite book |last=Ten Grotenhuis |first=Elizabeth |title=Japanese mandalas : representations of sacred geography |date=1999 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=0824820002 |oclc=39181008}}</ref>
== Origins and Etymology == Himegami means "female kami" or "goddess" in Japanese. They are important in Japanese mythology and Shinto religion. They often appear with male kami (hikogami) or as mothers to offspring kami (mikogami, 御子神).<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
== Kasuga-Hiraoka Himegami == The [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga]]-[[Hiraoka Shrine|Hiraoka]] Himegami is a prominent one.<ref name="Yoshiki" /><ref name="Okada" />
[[Hiraoka Shrine]] is located in the western foothills of the {{ill|Mount Ikoma|en|Mount Ikoma|qid=Q996259|lt=Ikoma Mountains|1=Mount Ikoma}} in central Osaka Prefecture. In its earliest days, it was a center for {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} and the ''[[kami]]'' of Kozudake, the peak immediately behind the shrine, came to be identified with [[Ame-no-Koyane]], the tutelary deity of the [[Nakatomi clan]], the ancestors of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. There is no documentary evidence of when the shrine was first constructed, but it is believed to be sometime during the [[Kofun period]]. When the Fujiwara built [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, two of the four ''kami'' enshrined (Ame-no-Koyomi and Himegami) were ''[[bunrei]]'' transferred from this shrine, and the [[Hiraoka Shrine]] was thus given the name of ''Moto-Kasuga'' ("former Kasuga").<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="Okada">{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
The four main kami enshrined at [[Kasuga-taisha]] are [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no koyane]], Himegami, [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no mikoto]], and [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no mikoto]].<ref>{{Cite book |author=Grapard, Allan G. |title=The protocol of the gods: a study of the Kasuga cult in Japanese history |date=1992 |publisher=University of California Press |isbn=0520070976 |oclc=25873140}}</ref> Though these are the primary divine beings of Kasuga taisha, they are often grouped together as a [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretic]], combined deity known as Kasuga {{ill|Myōjin|en|Myōjin|qid=Q5369590|lt=Daimyōjin|1=Myōjin}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tyler |first=Royall |title=The miracles of the Kasuga deity |date=2016 |publisher=Columbia University Press |isbn=9780231534765 |oclc=954193203}}</ref> Kasuga Daimyōjin is composed of five divine beings and each consists of a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} deity and Shinto kami counterpart. The fifth deity, [[Ame-no-Oshikumone]], was added much later and is said to be the divine child of [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no koyane]] and Himegami.<ref name="ten-grotenhuis-1999a" /> The importance of the multifaceted kami was that it became a template for future worshipers who wanted to combine several deities to pray to at once.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese mythology |date=2011 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |isbn=9781849728560 |oclc=755870995}}</ref>
The four main [[kami]] each have a shrine devoted to them which are all in the same [[Kasuga-zukuri|architectural style]]. They are characterized by sloping gabled roofs, a rectangular structure, {{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|qid=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}} (decorative logs), and {{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|qid=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}} (forked roof structures).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |title=Shinto shrines: a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion |date=2013 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=9780824837136 |oclc=1043096844}}</ref> The first hall established is dedicated to [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no mikoto]], the second to [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no mikoto]], the third to [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoyane no mikoto]], and the final hall is attributed to the consort, Himegami.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Main Sanctuary |url=http://www.kasugataisha.or.jp/guidance/map3/k_map01.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190624041142/http://www.kasugataisha.or.jp/guidance/map3/k_map01.html |archive-date=2019-06-24 |access-date=2019-03-28 |website=www.kasugataisha.or.jp}}</ref>
== Motifs ==
=== Triadic representation === Mount Kishimayama has three peaks. Each peak represents a different kami. One is a male kami. Another is a female kami. The third is an offspring kami.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
=== Consort and maternal roles === Himegami are usually wives to male kami. For example, [[Wakasahiko Shrine]] in Fukui worships a male and a female kami. [[Samukawa Shrine]] in Kanagawa does the same. At [[Shimogamo Shrine|Kamo Mioya Shrine]], a himegami is both a wife and a mother.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
=== Independent worship === Some himegami are not linked to male or offspring kami. For example, the himegami at [[Hirano Shrine]] might be the mother of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. These himegami are often seen as oracles.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
== Shrines == Many shrines include himegami. But their origins are often not clear. Shrines like [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]], {{ill|Ōharano Shrine|en|Ōharano Shrine|qid=Q3594020|lt=Ōharano Shrine|1=Ōharano Shrine}}, and [[Hiraoka Shrine]] are examples.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
=== Distribution across Japan === The [[Engishiki]] lists himegami shrines in several provinces. They are in {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|qid=Q1047117|lt=Izumi|1=Izumi Province}}, {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|qid=Q666514|lt=Echigo|1=Echigo Province}}, and {{ill|Buzen Province|en|Buzen Province|qid=Q1047134|lt=Buzen|1=Buzen Province}}. There are more shrines in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu|1=Settsu Province}}, {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato Province}}, and {{ill|Izu Province|en|Izu Province|qid=Q860928|lt=Izu provinces|1=Izu Province}}.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
=== List of shrines === Here shrines are presented
* [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga]]-[[Hiraoka Shrine|Hiraoka]] Himegami<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" /> ** [[Kasuga-taisha]]<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" /> ** [[Hiraoka Shrine]]<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" /> * {{ill|Ōharano Shrine|en|Ōharano Shrine|qid=Q3594020|lt=Ōharano Shrine|1=Ōharano Shrine}}<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" /> * [[Hirano Shrine]]<ref name="webarchiveorg-2022a" />
==References== {{reflist}}
